thread.section_title: Live Feed Wire - Golf News Today - EIN Presswire

author: Adrian Walker
text:
ADRIAN WALKER Tax-bill delay is just the latest example of our broken Massachusetts Legislature Email to a Friend
Share on Facebook
Share on Twitter
Print this Article
View Comments Another day, and another reason to resent this edition of the Massachusetts Legislature.
Friday morning, a state representative from Norwell employed a tactical delay to block a commercial property tax increase desperately sought by Mayor Michelle Wu.
Representative David DeCoste, a South Shore Republican who does not represent one square inch of Boston, blocked a home-rule petition that would allow the city to increase tax rates on commercial real estate for three years. The measure is intended to blunt what would otherwise be a sharp increase in residential tax bills beginning in January.
DeCoste is able to singlehandedly block the bill because of a procedural quirk — it is being taken up in informal session, during which much of the Legislature is absent from the building.
This bill is pending partly because this issue was one of dozens the Legislature failed to resolve during the chaotic regular session that wrapped up two months ago. Major legislation was left to be sorted out later — and the sorting has been predictably messy.
DeCoste may be using a minor parliamentary maneuver to get his way. But he has made it clear that he believes the tax hike is a bad idea. He told reporters Thursday — after he blocked the bill the first time — that he believes it will be bad for business. (He blocked it again on Friday.)
Advertisement
“The City of Boston drives the economic engine of the Commonwealth, and what they’re doing — what they’re proposing — in my estimation, is going to be devastating to the local commercial real estate industry,” he said.
Did I mention that he doesn’t represent Boston? Every Boston-based member of the House is for the bill. Even the business interests came around — grudgingly, to be sure — to support it. But, at least for now, that’s not enough to get it passed.
Even by State House standards, this bill has had a tortured path. Wu’s original proposal, many months ago, drew a cold reception on Beacon Hill. Tax increases are seldom popular, and the initial pitch for this increase didn’t move many lawmakers. For quite a while, it was presumed to be dead in the water.
Advertisement
After some reworking — and energetic lobbying by Wu and her closest Beacon Hill allies, Representatives Aaron Michlewicz and Mike Moran — it was resurrected late in the session, and a revamped version passed the House. But Senate President Karen Spilka sided with business leaders who didn’t care for the House version. A compromise was finally reached, but it still has to be voted on.
Time is of the essence: It has to pass before tax rates are set, which is why it can’t wait for the new session that begins early next year.
One could argue that there’s blame to go around for this slow-moving bill. The pandemic-driven decline in the value of commercial real estate has been clear for some time. And it was obvious that if taxes on those properties were going to continue to make up the huge share of city revenue that they do presently — about 70 percent — the rate would have to go up.
Otherwise — barring deep cuts in the city budget — that burden would be shifted to homeowners. That’s a solution nobody likes. This was a crisis looming in plain sight.
I think it’s fair to say that everyone involved could have acted with a greater sense of urgency. It took much longer than it should have to reach a compromise the city and the Legislature could both live with.
Still, this impasse is a product of the Legislature’s fundamental inability to handle its business. Huge pieces of legislation were left to be resolved until the session was near its close, until there simply wasn’t time to get them done. The end of any session is chaotic, but the zealously-observed tradition of disorder was taken to a simply unpardonable level.
